subject: literacy
title: Grade 5 Literacy
description: Essay structure, making inferences, and persuasive writing.

lessons.title: Essay Structure
lessons.objective: Learn how to plan and write a structured essay.
lessons.content:
A well-structured essay has THREE main parts:

📌 INTRODUCTION
  • Hook: an interesting opening sentence to grab attention
  • Background: briefly explain the topic
  • Thesis: your main argument or point

📝 BODY PARAGRAPHS (usually 2-3)
  Each paragraph should have:
  • Topic sentence: the main point of that paragraph
  • Evidence: facts, examples, or quotes
  • Explanation: explain how the evidence supports your point
  • Linking sentence: connect to the next paragraph

🏁 CONCLUSION
  • Restate your thesis (in different words)
  • Summarise your main points
  • End with a final thought or call to action

Planning tip: Use a MIND MAP or BULLET POINTS before you start writing!

exercises.question: What should the introduction of an essay contain?
exercises.hint: The introduction introduces your topic and your main argument.
exercises.explanation: An introduction should have a hook (to grab attention), background (context), and a thesis (your main argument or point).

exercises.question: The main argument or point of an essay is called the ______. (one word)
exercises.hint: It's a statement that tells the reader exactly what you'll argue.
exercises.explanation: The thesis is your main argument or claim. It tells the reader what your essay is about and what point you'll make.

lessons.title: Making Inferences
lessons.objective: Learn to read between the lines and make inferences.
lessons.content:
An INFERENCE is when you work out something that isn't directly stated.

You use CLUES from the text + your OWN KNOWLEDGE = INFERENCE

🔍 Example:
  Text: 'Maria grabbed her umbrella as she looked out the window.'
  Clue: She's getting her umbrella
  Your knowledge: Umbrellas are used when it rains
  Inference: It's probably raining (even though the text doesn't say so!)

📖 Good readers always infer:
  • How characters FEEL (from their actions and words)
  • WHY things happen (cause and effect)
  • What might happen NEXT (predictions)

🔑 Step-by-step:
  1. Find the CLUES in the text
  2. Think about what you already KNOW
  3. Put them together to make your INFERENCE
  4. Check: does your inference make sense?

exercises.question: 'Tom slammed his book shut and stomped out of the room.' How is Tom most likely feeling?
exercises.hint: Look at the action words: 'slammed' and 'stomped' — what emotion do they suggest?
exercises.explanation: The words 'slammed' and 'stomped' suggest strong, aggressive actions. We can infer that Tom is angry or frustrated!

exercises.question: Making an inference means:
options: Using clues and knowledge to work out unstated information
exercises.correctAnswer: Using clues and knowledge to work out unstated information
exercises.hint: It's like being a detective — using evidence to figure things out.
exercises.explanation: Making an inference means combining clues from the text with your own knowledge to understand things that aren't directly stated.
